column1: Malpractice
Passing mutable lists or dictionaries as default arguments can have unforeseen consequences. The persistent object is reused across function calls.
Bare except clauses hide actual programming errors and lose information about the error.
Without 'with', files may not be properly closed if exceptions occur, leading to memory issues or file corruption.
__init__ should only initialize instance members, not return values.
Python requires consistent indentation. Mixing spaces and tabs causes syntax errors and readability issues.
Manual key checking is verbose. dict.get() provides cleaner default value handling.
More specific exceptions must come before base classes, otherwise they become unreachable.
Manual dictionary initialization is verbose. defaultdict provides automatic default values.
super() requires the child class name as first argument and self as second for proper inheritance.
Methods that don't use self or cls should be functions or static methods.
Context managers must implement __exit__ with all required parameters (self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback).
Instance methods must include 'self' as first parameter to access instance members.
__future__ imports must be at the very top of the module to work correctly.
Python favors direct attribute access. Use properties only when computation is needed.
Overwriting built-in functions (like list, dict) breaks functionality elsewhere.
Protected members (_prefix) are intended for internal use only and may change.
Lambdas are for anonymous functions. Use def for named functions.
Functions should return consistent types. Use exceptions for error cases.
Wildcard imports pollute namespace and make code hard to trace.
Single-letter variable names make code hard to understand and maintain.
Global variables cause bugs from multiple functions accessing shared state.
Function names should be lowercase with underscores per PEP 8.
Manual index iteration is verbose. zip() provides cleaner parallel iteration.
Tuple unpacking is more concise and readable.
Use 'is' for None comparison, not '=='.
isinstance() is preferred over type() for type checking.
Explicit comparison to True is redundant.
exec() executes arbitrary strings as code and is unsafe.
Using dictionary keys in format strings improves clarity.
Lists are slow for membership checks compared to sets.
items() is more efficient and readable than key-based access.
Loop else executes even if no meaningful condition was met unless controlled by break.
Avoid bare 'except' catching all exceptions - this hides errors and makes debugging impossible
Do not silently ignore exceptions - this hides critical errors
Avoid catching generic Exception; use specific exceptions instead
Exceptions should be logged or handled explicitly, not silently ignored
Re-raising exceptions should preserve context to maintain debugging information
Do not use exceptions for normal control flow - use conditionals instead
Use 'raise ... from e' to preserve original exception traceback
Do not use mutable lists as default arguments - they persist across calls
Do not use mutable dicts as default arguments - they persist across calls
Do not use mutable sets as default arguments - they persist across calls
Default arguments should not call functions - they execute at module load time
Do not access index equal to array length - causes IndexError
Ensure loops include all intended elements
Check slicing boundaries carefully to avoid missing or extra characters
High index should be len(array)-1, not len(array)
Upper bound should be exclusive (<), not inclusive (<=)
Function has too many nested conditions - refactor into smaller functions
Too many conditional branches reduce readability and maintainability
Function does too many things - violates single responsibility principle
Function has too many parameters - use config object instead
Remove unused imports - they clutter code and slow startup
Function parameters should be used or removed
Variables declared but never used should be removed
Code after return/raise is never executed
Condition branch can never be reached due to ordering
Function defined but never used should be removed
Remove commented-out code instead of keeping it - use version control
Avoid hardcoded numeric literals - use named constants
Never hardcode passwords, tokens, or secrets in source code
Avoid hardcoded timeout values - make them configurable
Do not hardcode IPs or URLs - use configuration
Avoid hardcoded file system paths - use configuration
Use consistent naming style following PEP 8
Use meaningful variable/function names that describe purpose
Avoid single-letter names except for simple loop counters
Avoid similar variable names in same scope that can be confused
Class names should use CapWords/PascalCase convention
Function names should use snake_case convention
Constants should be UPPER_CASE with underscores
Do not override built-in function names
Too many nested if statements reduce readability
Nested if statements with same condition can be combined
Boolean return can be simplified to direct condition
Same condition repeated in multiple branches should be consolidated
Explicit True/False comparison is redundant
Using shell commands with user input creates injection vulnerabilities
shell=True with user input allows command injection
eval() on user input executes arbitrary code
MD5 and SHA1 are broken for security purposes
Plain or weakly hashed passwords are a security risk
random module is not suitable for security purposes
Time-based or sequential tokens are guessable
ECB mode encryption is insecure - use authenticated encryption
Direct string equality for secrets is vulnerable to timing attacks
Fixed or predictable IVs weaken encryption security
Do not use mutable data structures for argument defaults
Do not catch blind exception

Unnecessary comprehension (rewrite using constructor)

Return condition directly instead of if-else
Use contextlib.suppress instead of try-except-pass
Use ternary operator instead of if-else-block
Combine if branches using logical or operator
Use single with statement with multiple contexts
Use 'key in dict' instead of 'key in dict.keys()'
Do not explicitly return None
Missing explicit return at end of function
Unnecessary else after return statement

Use Path with / operator instead of os.path.join()
Use Path.open() instead of builtin open()
Use modern type annotations (list instead of List)
Use X | Y syntax for unions

Call on root logger (use module logger instead)

Useless expression (assign or remove)

If test is a tuple (always True)

Class name should use CapWords convention
Function name should be lowercase
Argument name should be lowercase
First argument of class method should be named cls
First argument of method should be named self
Variable in function should be lowercase
Exception name should have Error suffix
Use list comprehension instead of manual for loop
Use list() or list.copy() to create a copy
Use dictionary comprehension instead of manual for loop
No builtin eval() allowed
warn is deprecated in favor of warning
Use specific rule codes when ignoring type issues
Use specific rule codes when using noqa
Mock method should be called
Use regular assert instead of unittest-style assertion
pytest.raises() is too broad (use match parameter)
pytest.raises() block should contain single statement
Assertion should be broken down into multiple parts
No teardown in fixture (use return instead of yield)
Double quotes preferred (or single quotes if configured)
Change outer quotes to avoid escaping inner quotes
Unnecessary parentheses on raised exception
Unused noqa directive
Use of assert detected (can be optimized away)
Insecure usage of temporary file/directory
try-except-pass detected (log the exception)
Request call without timeout
tarfile.extractall() without member filtering
pickle insecure for untrusted data
Standard random not suitable for cryptographic purposes
Unsafe yaml.load() (use yaml.safe_load)
subprocess with shell=True (security risk)
subprocess call with untrusted input
Partial executable path (security risk)
SQL injection risk with string-based query
Jinja2 with autoescape=False (XSS risk)
Create your own exception (don't raise vanilla Exception)
Avoid long messages outside exception class
Prefer TypeError for invalid type
Use raise from to specify exception cause
Consider moving code to else block
Abstract raise to inner function
Unnecessary parentheses after class definition
Use type keyword for type aliases
Do not use informal or non-descriptive variable names in production or formal codebases; names should clearly express intent.
Do not leave debug print statements such as 'here', 'here1', or 'does this work?' in production code.
Avoid inefficient sorting approaches when built-in optimized sorting functions are available.
Avoid excessive nested loops, especially deeply nested for-loops, as they significantly reduce readability and performance.
Do not create variables, functions, or structures that are never used in the final code.
Do not define functions that do not return anything unless their purpose is explicitly side effects like logging or I/O.
Avoid reusing the same variable name in overlapping scopes where it can lead to confusion or unintended overwrites.
Do not leave commented-out debugging code or old logic scattered throughout production files.
Avoid overly complex one-line expressions that sacrifice readability for brevity.
Do not mix concerns inside a single function; each function should ideally have a single responsibility.
Avoid magic numbers; always replace them with named constants for clarity and maintainability.
Do not hardcode temporary test values directly into production logic.
Avoid excessive indentation levels that make code hard to follow visually.
Do not ignore error handling; silent failures make debugging extremely difficult.
Avoid inconsistent formatting or style within the same codebase.
Do not duplicate logic across multiple functions when it can be abstracted into a shared utility.
Avoid using misleading or overly generic function names that do not describe their behavior.
Do not leave experimental or half-finished code paths in production branches.
Avoid redundant checks that are already guaranteed by prior logic.
Do not overuse global state, as it makes code harder to test and debug.
Avoid mixing debugging artifacts with production logic (e.g., temporary logs or test flags).

Using `except: pass` and then wondering why bugs are impossible to find

Naming a dictionary `d` and then writing `d['d']` two lines later like a riddle
Writing `len(my_list) > 0` instead of just `if my_list:` because you forgot Pythonic style

Adding emoji log messages like logging.info('User authenticated ') in production
Creating a variable called `final_version` and then making 15 changes after it
Writing `except Exception as e: print(e)` and calling it error handling
Using `range(len(arr))` and then using `arr[i]` when you could just iterate directly
Defining a default argument as `[]` and then being surprised when it keeps growing

Creating five different functions that all do almost the same thing with tiny variations
Naming a boolean variable `flag` without explaining what the flag represents
Using `type(x) == type(y)` instead of `isinstance()` because you hate inheritance
Writing a function that takes 12 arguments and thinking 'this is fine'

Writing `if x: return True else: return False` instead of `return x`
